perturbation.original_text: Any materials or doc uments of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be promptly re turned within five (5) business days, accompanied by copies of such documentation, after the evaluatio n set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials or documents of which have been furnished by the Discloser to the Recipient will be returned within 6 months, without copies, after the evaluation set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement has been concluded.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return deadline to 6 months and removing the requirement for copies contradicts standard business practice and potentially legal expectations of prompt return and documentation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a statute of limitations violation, delaying the return of materials for 6 months without copies weakens the Discloser's position should a breach of contract claim arise. The statute of limitations in New York for breach of contract is six years. Waiting six months increases the risk of evidence being lost or damaged, complicating any future legal action. Prompt return of materials is essential for preserving evidence and mitigating damages.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Law § 213 (2022) - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The original text requires materials to be returned within five business days with copies. The changed text extends the return deadline to six months and removes the copies requirement. The Law Explanation highlights that delaying the return of materials for six months without copies weakens the Discloser's position in case of a breach. While the scraped snippets primarily concern the statute of limitations for contract breaches in New York (six years, as stated in both snippets), the Perturbation Explanation states that the change contradicts standard business practice. The six-month delay and removal of documentation could be seen as undermining the prompt preservation of evidence, contradicting the intent of the original agreement, even if it doesn't directly violate the statute of limitations.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be and mailed by certified mail return receipt requested or by Federal Express to the party to be notified at its address listed above (or at such different addresses as the party to receive the notice so designates by written notice to the other party).
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications hereunder may be delivered orally.  The recipient is not required to acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring only oral notice and eliminating the need for acknowledgment contradicts standard legal practice for contracts and increases the risk of disputes over whether notice was actually given.
perturbation.law_explanation: While a non-disclosure agreement is not specifically required to be in writing under the Statute of Frauds in New York, eliminating written notice and acknowledgement creates ambiguity and evidentiary problems regarding material terms or breaches, hindering enforcement of the agreement and contradicting the spirit of contract law, which requires clear and demonstrable agreement between parties.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-701 Agreements required to be in writing General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 7 § 5-701. Agreements required to be in writing. a. Every agreement, promise or undertaking is void, unless it or some note or memorandum thereof be in writing, and subscribed by the party to be charged therewith, or by his lawful agent, if such agreement, promise or undertaking: 1. By its terms is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof or the performance of which is not to be completed before the end of a lifetime; 2. Is a special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another person; 3. Is made in consideration of marriage, except mutual promises to marry; 5. Is a subsequent or new promise to pay a debt discharged in bankruptcy; 6. Notwithstanding section 2-201 of the uniform commercial code, if the goods be sold at public auction, and the auctioneer at the time of the sale, enters in a sale book, a memorandum specifying the nature and price of the property sold, the terms of the sale, the name of the purchaser, and the name of the person on whose account the sale was made, such memorandum is equivalent in effect to a note of the contract or sale, subscribed by the party to be charged therewith; 9. Is a contract to assign or an assignment, with or without consideration to the promisor, of a life or health or accident insurance policy, or a promise, with or without consideration to the promisor, to name a beneficiary of any such policy. This provision shall not apply to a policy of industrial life or health or accident insurance. 10. Is a contract to pay compensation for services rendered in negotiating a loan, or in negotiating the purchase, sale, exchange, renting or leasing of any real estate or interest therein, or of a business opportunity, business, its good will, inventory, fixtures or an interest therein, including a majority of the voting stock interest in a corporation and including the creating of a partnership interest.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates written notices, while the modified version permits oral notices and eliminates acknowledgment of receipt. This contradicts the standard legal practice and creates ambiguity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, NYS General Obligations Law § 5-701, requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable (such as those not performed within one year), the Law Explanation indicates that while a non-disclosure agreement isn't explicitly required to be written, the elimination of written notice and acknowledgment presents evidentiary problems. The shift to oral notice increases the risk of disputes and weakens the enforceability of the contract by making it harder to prove notice was given.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that export controlled information shall not be disclosed to any unauthorized foreign person, firm, country, incl uding foreign persons employed by Discloser. Recipient understands there are civil and criminal penalties for failure to comply with U.S. Export Control laws and will comply with all applicable laws while performing services for Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that export controlled information shall not be disclosed to any unauthorized foreign person, firm, country, incl uding foreign persons employed by Discloser. Recipient is not responsible for understanding or complying with U.S. Export Control laws while performing services for Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Stating the recipient is not responsible for understanding or complying with U.S. Export Control Laws directly contradicts those laws and regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 CFR § 732.3(b) outlines steps concerning the export control requirements. By stating that the Recipient is not responsible for understanding or complying with U.S. Export Control laws, the modified text directly contradicts the EAR, which places the onus on individuals and entities involved in export activities to comply with its regulations. This includes taking steps to determine if a license is required and adhering to all applicable restrictions.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that the recipient is 'not responsible for understanding or complying with U.S. Export Control laws,' directly contradicting the general principle that individuals and entities involved in export activities are expected to comply with export control regulations. The Law Explanation states that 15 CFR § 732.3(b) places the onus on individuals and entities involved in export activities to comply with its regulations, and the changed text eliminates this responsibility. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 15 CFR Part 732 which outlines steps for using EAR, which implies a responsibility to understand and comply. This contradiction is explicit and undermines a core legal obligation, thus warranting a 'YES'.

perturbation.original_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a peri od of five (5) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The foregoing commitments shall survive any termination of discussions between the parties, and shall continue for a period of twenty (20) years following the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the confidentiality period to 20 years may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable in some jurisdictions, especially if the information loses its value over time or becomes publicly available, thus potentially contradicting principles of contract law regarding reasonable restraints of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct statutory violation, a 20-year confidentiality period could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187 if the duration is longer than necessary to protect the Discloser's legitimate interests.  The Restatement articulates widely accepted principles of contract law, and courts often consider it. A court might find that such a long period is unduly burdensome on the Recipient, especially if the information becomes obsolete or public sooner, rendering the agreement unenforceable to that extent.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the confidentiality period from 5 years to 20 years. The Law Explanation argues this could be an unreasonable restraint of trade, referencing Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 187. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, stating a 20-year period may be unenforceable if the information loses value or becomes public. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Snippet 2 does not directly address restraint of trade or confidentiality periods, the LLM Law Explanation is persuasive given the other information provided, and this qualifies as a legal contradiction, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
